metadata.notes: Participation in IETF meetings constitutes agreement to the Note Well, which permits recording, transcription, and public archival of proceedings. See also BCP 78 (RFC 5378) and BCP 79 (RFC 8179) for IPR policies.

So there was a minor change to the fixed version search algorithm so this is the algorithm that users use to look for a specific version of a label.
And basically previously whenever we would be doing our search to the tree and we hit an expired log entry we would request a binary letter from that log in from the pre-extreated log entry.

And we would ask where does their search go from there and if our search would continue further into the expired portion of the tree from that log entry then we would have worth the search we would return error saying that this label version is expired.
But if our search would continue away from that out is the expired portion of the tree then we would continue our search as normal.

That was nice because it gave us a relatively simple definition of when a version of the label expires so we would just say like a label version expires whenever the log entry that it was inserted in passes the next one lifetime and it's not the greatest version like we talked about.
The downside of that though was just that it required keeping around basically a logarithmic number of the prefix trees from expired log entries.
So that was updated and.
Now whenever we're doing our search and we hit an expired log entry we just always immediately proceed to the rich child we basically always pretend that.
We got our binary search and we want to go to the right from there.
And then once our search is complete we look back at all of the log entries we went through and we say okay given the expired log entries if any that we hit that word and our search.

That word and our search path but we didn't look at them even though we should have did we still get a conclusive search result and specifically the case we used to determine that is if we are to the right of any unexpired distinguished log entry.
And that's because we know that the distinguished log entries are all inspected by the label owners so we know that whatever's in those are good and whatever is to the right of those is also good.
And if so then we can return success and if not then we return an error saying that the label version was expired.
So that has the benefit of making it much simpler to implement pretty because once a log entry passes its maximum lifetime we can guarantee that we really don't ever need it again.
It also makes it possible to actually guarantee when things will be deleted because we're not holding things around past their maximum lifetime so that's also really important for.
Compliance stuff that I was just talking about like actually making sure that stuff is deleted within a fixed amount of time.
